flashcards.back: A special member function that initializes objects when they are created. It has the same name as the class and no return type.
A special member function that cleans up resources when an object is destroyed. It has the same name as the class preceded by a tilde (~) and no parameters or return type.

flashcards.front: What are the three main types of constructors?
flashcards.back:
1. Default constructor (no parameters)
2. Parameterized constructor (with parameters)
3. Copy constructor (creates copy of existing object)

flashcards.front: When is a destructor automatically called?
flashcards.back: When an object goes out of scope, when delete is called on a dynamically allocated object, or when the program terminates.
Defining multiple constructors with different parameter lists, allowing objects to be initialized in different ways.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of constructor initialization lists?
flashcards.back: To efficiently initialize const members, reference members, and base classes before the constructor body executes.

flashcards.front: What is the Rule of Three?
flashcards.back: If a class requires a user-defined destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment operator, it likely requires all three.

flashcards.front: Why should copy constructor parameters be passed by reference?
flashcards.back: To avoid infinite recursion that would occur if passed by value (which would require calling the copy constructor again).

flashcards.front: In what order are constructors and destructors called for multiple objects?
flashcards.back: Constructors are called in the order of object creation, destructors are called in reverse order (LIFO).

flashcards.front: When would you use a virtual destructor?
flashcards.back: In a base class when you plan to derive other classes from it and use polymorphism, to ensure proper cleanup of derived objects.
